text.tspan: HEALTH ENDPOINT: every service exposes an HTTP endpoint
that reports its current state. Used by load balancers,
Kubernetes, monitors, and load-shedding logic.
LIVENESS
GET /healthz/live u2192 200 OK or 5xx
READINESS
GET /healthz/ready u2192 200 OK or 503
STARTUP
GET /healthz/startup u2192 200 OK once warm
DEEP HEALTH (custom)
GET /healthz/deep u2192 detailed JSON
CONSUMERS
u2022 Kubernetes / Nomad orchestrators (liveness / readiness / startup probes)
u2022 Load balancers (use readiness/health to decide whether to send traffic)
u2022 Service mesh (Envoy uses health for outlier detection)
u2022 External monitors (Pingdom, Datadog Synthetics, internal)
u2022 Status pages / human dashboards
Q: 'Am I broken so badly the process should be RESTARTED?'
Returns OK unless the process is genuinely deadlocked / corrupt.
Does NOT check dependencies.
Fails u2192 K8s kills and restarts the pod.
MUST be cheap and almost always OK u2014 wrongly failing = restart loop.
Q: 'Am I ready to ACCEPT TRAFFIC?'
Checks critical dependencies (DB connection, config loaded, warmup done).
Fails u2192 K8s removes pod from Service endpoints (no new traffic);
existing traffic continues to drain.
Fails on intermittent dep blip u2014 NOT a restart, just a traffic pause.
More strict than liveness: temporarily failing is normal during startup / draining / dep outage.
Q: 'Have I finished WARMING UP?' (slow-start apps: JVM, large model load)
Liveness and readiness checks gated until startup OK.
Until first OK, the pod gets MORE TIME to start without being killed.
Once OK, falls back to liveness/readiness for ongoing checks.
Q: 'What's the state of every dependency?'
Used by humans / external monitors, NOT by orchestrators.
'database': {'status': 'ok', 'latency_ms': 5},
'redis': {'status': 'degraded', 'last_error': 'timeout'},
'kafka': {'status': 'ok'},
'version': 'v2.4.1'
NEVER use deep checks for liveness u2014 deps go down momentarily,
don't want to restart the pod every time.
